passage:
English verbs change a little with the person: I speak, you speak, he speakS.
French does the same thing, just more visibly:
je parle · tu parles · il parle · nous parlons · vous parlez · ils parlent
(the first three SOUND identical — only spelling changes!)
This changing is called CONJUGATION.
Surprise: you already know many conjugated forms as chunks — je suis (I am), j'ai (I have), je vais (I go), je veux (I want), je peux (I can). You've been conjugating for a week without knowing it.
options: The verb changing its form to match the person (je / tu / il…)
A list of words to memorize alphabetically
explain: That's all it is. When a drill says “je ___ (être)”, it asks: what's the je-form of être? You already know it: suis.

prompt: “Je suis” (I am) is the je-form of which verb?
explain: je suis = the “I” shape of être. The chunk you know IS the conjugation.

prompt: “J'ai” (I have) is the je-form of which verb?
explain: je + ai squeezes into j'ai. Infinitive: avoir.

prompt: “Je vais” (I go / I'm going) is the je-form of which verb?
explain: aller is very irregular: je vais, tu vas, il va. But you already own all three sounds from ça va !

hint: You say it every day: je suis ici.
explain: être: je suis, tu es, il est — fully irregular, but these three are the most heard words in French.
avoir: j'ai, tu as, il a. Also irregular — also everywhere.

hint: The chunk: je vais à Paris.
explain: aller: je vais, tu vas, il va.

prompt: Why do il/elle forms feel sneaky? il parle vs je parle…
options: They often SOUND the same as the je-form — only the spelling shifts
They are always totally different words
il-forms are only used in writing
explain: For -er verbs, je/tu/il forms all sound identical. Your EAR already knows them; your fingers are just learning the spelling.

prompt: From now on, when a drill asks for a form you haven't met…
options: read the hint above the box — every conjugation drill shows the verb's pattern first
explain: The hint carries the pattern (je veuX, tu veuX, il veuT), and Language Transfer will keep teaching these forms track by track. Drills are practice, not ambush — and now you know the word for what they practice.
